metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit ordering guard: either (a) make this story strictly dependent on the terraform adoption story having merged (so `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` exists at PR time), or (b) scope the runbook section to 'placeholder — fill in once sso-gate module is merged' and add an AC that the section is completed only after the module path is confirmed in the repo.
findings.finding: The plan's 'What' section asserts the SSO runbook will document 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the reusable `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` module — which the terraform story adopts into rebar as code alongside the auth host.' This is a codebase existence claim: the module is described as existing ('reusable', 'an existing module') at plan-execution time. A directory listing of infra/terraform/ shows no `modules/` subdirectory, and a search for 'sso-gate' in infra/terraform returns zero hits. The module does not exist in the repo, and the Scope section only says this story 'Depends on the terraform adoption story landing (or landing alongside)' — it does not guarantee the module will exist when the doc story executes. If it hasn't landed yet, the runbook section it anchors is unbacked.
findings.location: What section — SSO runbook bullet; Scope section
findings.suggested_fix: Add a hard dependency on the terraform adoption ticket in the Scope section and add an AC such as: 'The runbook's subdomain-protection section references `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` and the module exists at that path in the merged PR.' Alternatively, draft the runbook section with a clearly-marked `TODO: update path once terraform story lands` and exclude it from the runbook AC until then.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove or correct the claim that `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` exists as a ready-to-reference module — the sibling terraform story explicitly does NOT adopt sso-gate; either drop that runbook section, stub it as 'future work', or add a dependency on a story that actually creates the module.
findings.finding: The What section asserts the SSO runbook will document 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the reusable `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` module — which the terraform story adopts into rebar as code alongside the auth host.' However, no `infra/terraform/modules/` directory exists in the repo, and the sibling terraform story (7246-bd9e-58a3-41fd) explicitly states it 'Does NOT adopt snap's … audit-dashboard sso-gate.' The module will not exist when this story lands, making the runbook section's reference to an 'existing module' a hallucinated edit target.

findings.suggested_fix: Replace the runbook's sso-gate module reference with a note that subdomain SSO gating via a reusable module is future work (not yet adopted), and document only what the terraform story actually delivers (auth_host, auth_sso, acm, oidc resources in infra/terraform/).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a clearly-marked conditional note in the SSO runbook's sso-gate section (e.g. a `> **Note:** This module is added by the companion terraform adoption story; verify it exists under infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate before following these steps.`) so operators know the module is a pre-requisite that may not yet be present.
findings.finding: The SSO runbook (to be added under infra/runbooks/) references the reusable infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate module, but this module does not yet exist in the repository. findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm (or gate-on) the `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` module's existence before merging this doc story; if the terraform adoption story may not land simultaneously, add a conditional caveat in the runbook (e.g. 'module arrives with story X; until then, see snap's equivalent config at…') so the runbook is accurate regardless of landing order.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the SSO runbook will document 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the reusable `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` module — which the terraform story adopts into rebar as code alongside the auth host, so the runbook references an existing module.' findings.finding: The plan states the SSO runbook will document 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the reusable `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` module — which the terraform story adopts into rebar as code alongside the auth host.' findings.finding: The What section asserts the SSO runbook will reference 'an existing module' at infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate, but no such module exists anywhere in the repo (infra/terraform/ has no modules/ subdirectory; all sso-gate searches return zero hits). This is an unverified capability assertion: if the terraform story hasn't landed, the runbook section describing how to use sso-gate will reference a non-existent artifact.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either (a) gate this runbook section on the terraform adoption story actually landing (enforce ordering so `sso-gate` exists before this doc merges) or (b) write the 'protect a new subdomain' section without asserting the module already exists — e.g., 'once `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` lands, use it as follows…' — so the runbook is accurate regardless of ordering.
findings.finding: The runbook's 'What' section directs documenting 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the reusable `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` module' and asserts it is 'an existing module,' but no such module exists anywhere in the repo: `infra/terraform/` has no `modules/` subdirectory and a full-repo search for 'sso-gate' returns zero hits in any source file. The plan acknowledges this is contingent on 'the terraform adoption story landing (or landing alongside).' Authoring a runbook section that treats a non-existent, zero-call-site module as a stable, reusable abstraction is a premature-abstraction / YAGNI violation within the documentation scope.
findings.location: What section — 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the reusable `infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate` module — which the terraform story adopts into rebar as code alongside the auth host, so the runbook references an existing module'

findings.finding: The leaf's runbook 'What' promises to document 'how to protect a new rebar subdomain via the reusable infra/terraform/modules/sso-gate module — which the terraform story adopts into rebar as code alongside the auth host.' The sibling terraform story (7246) explicitly excluded the sso-gate: its Scope says 'Does NOT adopt snap's … audit-dashboard sso-gate', and the operator-attested plan/apply comment confirms it was intentionally dropped. The parent epic (d58f) also does not include sso-gate adoption. The leaf is documenting a module that the parent's plan does not contain — and that the sibling terraform story explicitly ruled out.

If sso-gate documentation is genuinely wanted, first add sso-gate adoption to the parent epic and the terraform sibling, then re-scope this leaf.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command/check to each AC — e.g., AC 2: `grep -v 'already exists in the account' infra/terraform/iam.tf`; AC 4: verify headings for architecture, secrets, rotation, and incident revoke exist with `grep -E 'Architecture|Secrets|Rotation|Incident' infra/runbooks/sso-operations.md`.
findings.finding: No acceptance criterion includes a concrete proving command or check. For documentation-only work the checks are simple file-content assertions (grep, ls, cat), but none are stated. An executing agent must infer what 'done looks like' rather than running a specified verification step.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all four bullets)
findings.suggested_fix:
Append a 'Verification' block to the ACs:
```
Verification:
- AC1: grep -i 'owns\|ownership' docs/gerrit-aws-setup.md returns ≥1 match covering OIDC provider + SSO stack.
- AC2: grep 'already exists in the account' infra/terraform/iam.tf exits non-zero (line removed).
- AC3: ls docs/adr/ lists the new ADR file; grep -iE 'adopt|tear.down|name.preserv|state.migr' docs/adr/<new-file>.md returns matches for all four decision points.
- AC4: grep -E '^#+.*(Architecture|Secrets|Rotation|Revoke)' infra/runbooks/sso-operations.md returns 4 matches.
```

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one concrete validation step to the runbook AC — e.g., 'A rebar team member has dry-run the rotation procedure against the live stack' or 'A peer on-call engineer has reviewed the runbook for correctness' — so the deliverable is operationally validated, not just topically complete.
findings.finding: The plan names rebar infra engineers as beneficiaries and states the problem (docs live in a dead repo), but no AC or step specifies a concrete validation mechanism to confirm the runbook is operationally correct — e.g., a dry-run against the live stack, a peer review by an on-call engineer, or a checklist item confirming each rotation procedure was verified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'An infra/runbooks/ SSO runbook covers architecture, secrets, rotation, and incident revoke.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: '[ ] A rebar infra team member has verified each rotation and incident-revoke step against the live stack (or a staging equivalent) and signed off on the runbook.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether the companion terraform story will add an `aws_iam_openid_connect_provider` resource to rebar's HCL; if yes, gate the iam.tf comment update on that resource landing; if no, revise the replacement comment to say rebar *consumes* (not *creates*) the provider, and update the ADR to accurately reflect the ownership boundary.
findings.finding: The iam.tf comment update claims rebar 'creates/owns' the OIDC provider, but no aws_iam_openid_connect_provider terraform resource exists anywhere in the codebase — only an IAM role that federates to the provider's ARN. If the companion terraform story does not land that resource, updating the comment to assert rebar creates the provider replaces one falsehood with another.
findings.location: What section ('Fix the stale comment in infra/terraform/iam.tf') + infra/terraform/iam.tf line 136
findings.suggested_fix: If the OIDC provider resource is not being adopted into rebar's terraform, replace the stale comment with: 'The OIDC provider (token.actions.githubusercontent.com) is managed/owned by rebar's AWS account (adopted from snap, see docs/adr/<new-ADR>); this role federates to it.' This is accurate regardless of whether a terraform resource exists.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit merge-order guard: either block this doc story on the terraform adoption story landing first, or add an AC that the OIDC comment fix ships in the same PR/commit as the `aws_iam_openid_connect_provider` resource block, so the documented ownership and the IaC management are always in sync.
findings.finding: The plan fixes the iam.tf comment to say rebar OWNS the GitHub OIDC provider, but no `aws_iam_openid_connect_provider` resource exists in rebar's Terraform (search confirms absence). The plan's Scope says 'depends on the terraform adoption story landing (or landing alongside),' but the dependency is not enforced — if this doc story merges before the terraform story, the comment will assert rebar owns the provider while no IaC actually manages it, creating a drift gap between documented ownership and actual resource management.
findings.location: infra/terraform/iam.tf line 136 / ## Scope / AC: 'iam.tf's OIDC comment no longer claims the provider is externally owned'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC: 'The iam.tf comment fix and the `aws_iam_openid_connect_provider` resource block land in the same merge.' Alternatively, add a `data "aws_iam_openid_connect_provider"` lookup (not a resource) to document the dependency explicitly while the adoption story is pending, and note in the comment that a full resource block is coming.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Scope the GHA drift-detection role's permissions to a purpose-built policy (S3 state bucket read + specific SSM paths + the exact EC2/EBS/Route53/CloudWatch data-source actions) rather than the account-wide `ReadOnlyAccess` managed policy, consistent with the least-privilege model the rest of iam.tf practices.
findings.finding: The GHA drift-detection IAM role (formally documented as rebar-owned by this plan) is attached to `ReadOnlyAccess` — a broad AWS-managed policy granting hundreds of `List*`/`Get*`/`Describe*` permissions across all AWS services account-wide. For a CI role whose sole purpose is running `terraform plan` (reading S3 state, SSM parameters, EC2/EBS/Route53/CloudWatch data sources), this violates the least-privilege principle the existing iam.tf comments explicitly champion ('NO wildcard on the whole account').
findings.location: infra/terraform/iam.tf lines 174-176 / T10(b) LEAST-PRIVILEGE IAM
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `ReadOnlyAccess` with a scoped inline policy: `s3:GetObject`/`s3:ListBucket` on the state bucket ARN, `ssm:GetParameter`/`ssm:GetParameters`/`ssm:GetParametersByPath` on `arn:aws:ssm:*:*:parameter/rebar/*`, and the specific `ec2:Describe*`/`route53:Get*`/`cloudwatch:Describe*` actions the data sources in infra/terraform/*.tf actually invoke.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to AC4 (and the runbook's What) a requirement that the incident-revoke-lever section declares its access posture: who may invoke it (specific IAM role/group), by what mechanism (e.g. AWS console → Lambda invoke, SSM Run Command, direct API), and with what authentication — public-with-rationale / authenticated-with-roles / internal-only.
findings.finding: The runbook's 'incident revoke lever' section has no declared access posture in the plan: the plan does not state that the runbook will document who can invoke the lever, via what mechanism (console, API call, Lambda invoke, SSM runcommand?), or with what authentication. For a boundary-crossing SSO control that can revoke all active sessions, an undeclared access posture is itself the failure — an operator reading the runbook has no guidance on whether the lever is protected, and an attacker who can invoke it can perform a denial-of-service against all SSO users.
findings.location: What section — 'incident revoke lever'; AC4 — 'An infra/runbooks/ SSO runbook covers architecture, secrets, rotation, and incident revoke'
findings.suggested_fix: In the runbook's incident-revoke section, add a box: 'Access posture: internal-only; invocable only by an IAM principal with [specific role/policy]; requires [MFA / session token / etc.]. Do NOT expose the invoke URL or API endpoint publicly.' Update AC4 to: 'covers architecture, secrets, rotation, incident revoke, AND declares the access class and auth requirement for each operational control.'

findings.finding: The 'What' section includes a retag note (`project=snap-demo` → `Project=rebar`) as a deliverable, but no Acceptance Criterion maps to it, so an executor has no way to verify this item is done and it could be silently skipped.

findings.finding: The retag change (`project=snap-demo` → `Project=rebar`) is listed in the 'What' section but absent from all four Acceptance Criteria, meaning it can be silently dropped without failing any AC check, leaving stale tag documentation.
findings.location: What section (retag bullet) vs. Acceptance Criteria (all four ACs)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a fifth AC: '`docs/gerrit-aws-setup.md` or the SSO runbook notes the resource retag from `project=snap-demo` to `Project=rebar` and its rationale.'

findings.finding: The 'What' section lists the retag notation (project=snap-demo → Project=rebar) as a deliverable, but no Acceptance Criterion verifies it, leaving it without an end-state-observable proof.
findings.location: What section (final bullet) vs. Acceptance Criteria (all four ACs)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to ACs: '- [ ] The re-homing ADR (or gerrit-aws-setup.md) records the retag from project=snap-demo to Project=rebar.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
